1: The vision of Ezekiel 8 occurs in the sixth year, sixth month, fifth day — September/October 592 BCE — fourteen months after the initial commission vision (Ezekiel 1:2). The elders of Judah sitting before Ezekiel in his exile house at Tel-abib represent the community's use of the prophet as an oracle-consultant — the normal function of a prophetic figure in the ANE world. The Spirit's transport of Ezekiel to Jerusalem (v3) is the vision-journey genre common in ancient apocalyptic literature, in which the seer is transported to see heavenly or distant realities.
2: The figure of fire from the loins downward and brightness upward — a theophanic appearance identical to the glory-figure of Ezekiel 1:27. The description in v2 anchors the vision firmly in the throne-chariot framework of Ezekiel 1: the guide is the glory-figure of YHWH himself, or a representation of it, conducting the vision-tour. The fiery lower half / bright upper half contrast may reflect ancient iconography of divinity: Mesopotamian and Canaanite divine figures are often depicted with flames at their base and light emanating from their heads.
3: The stretched-out hand and the transport to Jerusalem by the Spirit — the lock of hair (tsitsit roʾshi) by which Ezekiel is grabbed is a distinctive detail suggesting the physicality and urgency of the prophetic transport. The Spirit-transport formula here (wayyiqaḥ ʾoti betsissit roʾshi wayyissaʾ ʾoti ruaḥ) combines the hand-grasp with the Spirit-lift, distinguishing this entry into the vision from the prior episodes where only the Spirit lifts. The north gate of the inner court was the primary entrance for worshipers — the location of the "image of jealousy" means it was encountered by every worshiper entering the sanctuary precinct.
4: The glory of YHWH (kavod) appearing in the inner court — the divine presence that Ezekiel last saw at Tel-abib (Ezekiel 1-3) is now visible within the Jerusalem temple itself. The irony is devastating: YHWH is present in his own temple to witness the abominations being committed in it. The vision establishes that YHWH sees and knows what occurs in the temple, even the secret practices in hidden chambers (v12).
5: The image of jealousy (semel haqqinʾah) at the north gateway entrance — the noun semel (image/likeness) appears in Deuteronomy 4:16 for idols in general; combined with qinʾah (jealousy) it suggests the idol that provokes YHWH's jealousy. The leading candidates based on historical context are: (1) the Asherah pole installed by Manasseh (2 Kings 21:7), (2) a goddess-statue (possibly Asherah/Astarte) set up in the temple gate area, or (3) a cult image of Baal. The north gate location is significant because the north is associated with divine assembly in Canaanite mythology (Baal's mountain Zaphon = north).
6: The question "Do you see what they are doing — great abominations that drive me far from my sanctuary?" — the divine analysis is that the abominations actively drive YHWH away. This is the theological foundation for the glory-departure vision of Ezekiel 10-11: the idolatry does not merely coexist with YHWH's presence but is causally responsible for removing it. The spatial metaphor of "driving far" (lemarḥaqah meʿal miqdashi) is the inverse of the nearness-of-YHWH language of Deuteronomy.
7: The hole in the wall at the temple entrance — Ezekiel is told to dig through the wall to find the entrance to a hidden chamber. This detail grounds the vision in the architecture of the Jerusalem temple precinct. Archaeological excavations around the Temple Mount and in pre-exilic Jerusalem (the Ophel area) have revealed complex systems of storage rooms, side chambers, and administrative spaces adjoining the temple — the kind of spaces where secret priestly activity could occur out of public sight.
8: The digging through the wall — the same verb (ḥatar) used for the prince digging through the wall in the exile sign-act of Ezekiel 12:5, 7, 12 — creates an intertextual link: the secret access to the temple's idolatry mirrors the forced exit from Jerusalem in siege. Both involve digging through walls as illicit entries/exits.
9: "Go in and see the wicked abominations that they are doing here" — the progressive revelation structure of the vision (four increasingly shocking abominations shown in sequence) is a literary technique that builds theological case. The repetition of "you will see yet greater abominations" (vv6, 13, 15) creates the escalating horror of the prophetic disclosure.
10: The chambers filled with carvings of creeping things, abominable animals, and all the idols of the house of Israel — the wall-to-wall animal and idol imagery in the secret priestly chambers has been connected to several ANE traditions: (1) Egyptian animal iconography (crocodiles, ibises, scarabs) that appeared in the Hellenized Levant; (2) Mesopotamian protective animal imagery used in the apotropaic ritual traditions that came into Judah; (3) the animal cult imagery associated with the Asherah cult. The 70 elders performing this secret incense worship suggest a priestly college maintaining an alternative sacrificial tradition.
11: The seventy elders of the house of Israel burning incense — the number 70 is the complete council of elders (Exodus 24:1, 9; Numbers 11:16, 24-25). Jaazaniah son of Shaphan is specifically named: the Shaphan family were prominent royal officials under Josiah (2 Kings 22:3ff.) who supported the reform; a son of Shaphan practicing secret idolatry represents the complete failure of even the reform movement's legacy. The dark-room incense burning (be-ḥasheikh ish becheder maskhito, "each man in his room of images") echoes the widespread practice of household shrines and private devotional objects documented archaeologically in pre-exilic household archaeology.
12: "YHWH does not see us; YHWH has forsaken the land" — the theology behind the secret worship: the elders concluded that YHWH had abandoned the land (possibly after the first deportation of 597 BCE), making worship of other gods pragmatically necessary. This crisis-pluralism — seeking insurance from multiple divine patrons when one's primary deity seems to have failed — is well documented in the ancient world. The Lachish Letters (ostraca from the final years of Jerusalem) show a community at the breaking point of the old covenant certainties.
13: The third abomination: women weeping for Tammuz (Tammuz) at the north gate. Tammuz (Sumerian Dumuzid) was the Mesopotamian shepherd-deity whose annual death (into the underworld) was mourned in midsummer ritual. The Akkadian name Dumuzi/Tammuz appears in Babylonian religious texts from the third millennium onward; the fourth month of the Babylonian calendar was named after him, and his cult involved ritual lamentation by women, sacred marriage rites, and fertility themes. The presence of this cult at the Jerusalem temple gate reflects the penetration of Babylonian religious practices into Judean life during the Assyrian and Babylonian periods.
14: The location at the north gate entrance — the same gate where the image of jealousy stood (v5) — concentrates the abominations at the primary public entrance point. The north gate was both the main access to the inner court and, in Canaanite cosmology, the direction associated with divine assembly (Zaphon). The ritual lamentation for Tammuz at this gate combines Mesopotamian fertility religion with the sacred geography of the Jerusalem temple precinct.
15: The fourth and "greatest" abomination: twenty-five men at the inner court's east entrance, between the portico and the altar, with their backs to YHWH's temple, worshiping the sun toward the east. Solar worship in the pre-exilic Jerusalem cult is documented by the Horses of the Sun kept near the temple entrance (2 Kings 23:11, removed by Josiah). The positioning — backs to the temple, faces east — represents a deliberate liturgical inversion: the normal posture of temple worship faced west toward the Most Holy Place; these worshipers orient themselves toward the rising sun instead.
16: The precise location — between the ʾulam (portico) and the mizbeaḥ (altar) — is the priests' space, the zone accessible only to the priesthood. The twenty-five may correspond to the priestly courses or to a high-priestly council. Their sun-worship at the sacred heart of the priestly precinct represents the highest level of cultic corruption: the very men responsible for maintaining YHWH's worship are conducting a rival solar liturgy in the most sacred priestly space.
17: "Is it too light a thing for the house of Judah to commit the abominations they commit here, that they should fill the land with violence?" — YHWH's rhetorical question connects the cultic abominations of the temple with the social violence (ḥamas) condemned throughout the book. The "branch to the nose" (meshalḥim ʾet-hazzemorah ʾel-ʾappam) is obscure — it may refer to an offensive gesture in a ritual context, a Zoroastrian influence (holding a barsom bundle to the nose in sun worship), or a defiant gesture toward YHWH.
18: "Therefore I will act in wrath; my eye will not spare, nor will I have pity" — the non-compassion formula of v4 is repeated with the specific motivation of the cultic abominations now cataloged. The conclusion — "though they cry in my ears with a loud voice, I will not hear them" — is the reverse of the Shema: Israel's crying out will receive no divine response, because they cried out to their foreign gods rather than to YHWH. The refusal to hear corresponds to Israel's refusal to hear YHWH's word through the prophets.
